story:
My phone buzzed with Kristina’s text: Lane, I’d like to talk to you if you’re available. I have some exciting news! I was exhausted and not in the mood to talk, but ignoring her felt wrong—especially when “exciting” from Krissy usually meant something big. Like her engagement to Robert. Now broken. Because of me. That part still stung.

I joined the call. Kristina squealed when she saw me and announced her promotion—from paralegal to full-fledged attorney. It was huge. Ever since she finished at Columbia Law, she’d worked toward this. She was a natural leader, breaking through in a field that didn’t always make room for women like us. I was proud—and small at the same time, like the little sister hiding in her shadow again.

“Congrats, sis,” I said, meaning it. She was glowing, tears in her eyes. She wanted to celebrate, maybe even have it announced at church on Sunday. She asked if I could come home this weekend for a family dinner. I’d avoided her and our parents all summer. Guilt tugged at me. I told her I’d try to be there. She lit up.

She mentioned our aunts were flying in from London, the first time we’d all be together in years. That alone felt like reason enough to go. “I’ll be there for you, Krissy,” I promised, hoping I could keep it.

Before she signed off, I asked about Robert. Heat rose in my cheeks. I’d stayed away from home and away from the mess, and I hadn’t checked in on how she was coping. She reminded me he’d blocked her on social media, so there were no answers from him. Still, she’d stayed close with his sisters—Birgitte and the others—who treated her like family and avoided digging into the breakup. Angela was there for her too, helping her find respectful ways to handle other people’s questions. “You know how nosy church folks can be,” she joked, and I did.

Then she surprised me. “I’m using this time to focus on myself and trust God’s plans,” she said. She wasn’t desperate to hang on to something that wasn’t meant to be. In another life, maybe she and Robert would have married and had kids. But she believed God knew what He was doing. If Robert ever wanted a second chance, her door would be open—but she only wanted what was true and freely chosen. No chasing. No bitterness.

Listening to her hurt and her hope made my chest ache. Robert had been her first real love. And what I had with him could never compare to what they might have built together. The what-ifs crowded my head. I found myself thinking hard about the baby and what would be best. I didn’t want to build a life with Robert out of shame or pressure. If I couldn’t give this child the steady, loving home it deserved, I needed to consider adoption for the child’s sake—not to fix anyone’s relationship, but to choose a future that was honest and kind.

“Hey, you look lost in thought,” she said gently. I blamed schoolwork and schedules, and she laughed, nostalgic for Columbia. She told me she’d understand if I couldn’t come home, but that it would mean a lot if I did.

We said I love you and hung up. I’d learned how she was healing. And I knew it was time to face my own truth.
